title: Moroccan Harira Soup

subs.name: Yellow moong dal (cooks faster and is softer) or toor dal (gives a slightly denser texture)

ingredients.name: celery stalks, finely sliced
visual_checks: Skip if unavailable; add an extra onion instead
ingredients.notes: Skip if unavailable; add an extra onion instead

subs.name: Extra onion or 2 tbsp finely chopped spring onion; celery adds a subtle herbal note that parsley can partly replace

ingredients.name: thin wheat vermicelli (semiya), broken into 2 cm pieces

subs.name: Broken spaghetti or rice (add 3 tbsp raw rice instead); or leave it out for a gluten-free version
Any neutral cooking oil; the flavour change is minimal

steps.text: Heat oil in a heavy-bottomed pot over medium flame. Add the onion and celery and cook for 5 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the onion turns soft and translucent. Add the garlic and stir for 30 seconds until fragrant.

Add tomato paste and cook for 2 minutes, stirring, until it deepens in colour. Add the chopped fresh tomatoes, cinnamon, cumin, turmeric and black pepper. Stir well and cook for 3 minutes until the tomatoes start to break down.

Pour in 1.2 litres of water. Add the rinsed masoor dal, chickpeas and salt. Bring to a boil, skim off any foam, then reduce the flame to low, cover and simmer for 20 minutes until the lentils are completely soft and falling apart — this gives the soup its characteristic thick body.
Add the broken vermicelli to the soup. Simmer uncovered for 4–5 minutes until the pasta is just cooked — the soup will thicken noticeably. If it becomes too thick, add a splash of hot water.
Squeeze in the lemon juice. Taste and adjust salt. Ladle into bowls and finish with a generous scattering of fresh coriander and parsley. Serve with Moroccan bread or any flatbread on the side.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: vermicelli → Broken spaghetti or rice (add 3 tbsp raw rice instead); or leave it out for a gluten-free version; masoor-dal → Yellow moong dal (cooks faster and is softer) or toor dal (gives a slightly denser texture); celery → Extra onion or 2 tbsp finely chopped spring onion; celery adds a subtle herbal note that parsley can partly replace; olive-oil → Any neutral cooking oil; the flavour change is minimal
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Masoor dal replaces hard-to-find lentil varieties in the same step. Semiya (wheat vermicelli) is the exact Indian equivalent of the pasta used. The entire soup cooks in one pot with pantry spices and is well suited to a pressure cooker — cook on high pressure for 8 minutes (about 3 whistles) after adding the lentils, then add vermicelli and finish on the stovetop.
